questions.question: How do you measure success of the Hot Home feature in Redfin?

saraswati.real_test: The interviewer is testing whether you understand that measuring success of a prediction feature requires evaluating the prediction itself, not just user engagement with it.
saraswati.senior_distinction: A mid-level PM proposes engagement metrics. A senior PM starts by asking what the prediction accuracy is and whether it's being tracked at all — then builds the measurement framework from accuracy upward.
takeaways: For prediction features, measure accuracy of the prediction before engagement with it — engagement without accuracy is a vanity metric.
Separate stakeholder success metrics — buyer utility and seller satisfaction can conflict, and blending them hides the tension.
Measure trust decay through repeat behaviour — trust erodes slowly then breaks suddenly.

episodes.title: Measuring Redfin's Hot Home: Start Here, Not There
episodes.subtitle: Before you pick a metric, you need to understand what the feature actually claims — and why that changes everything.
episodes.youtube_description: Most PMs jump straight to click-through rate and tour bookings when asked to measure Redfin's Hot Home feature. This episode shows why that's the wrong starting point — and what question you should ask first about any prediction feature.

interactive.intro: The way you answer this question reveals how you think about prediction features. Make a choice at each step — the reasoning unlocks after you commit.

decision_points.prompt: Before defining any metrics — what's the first thing you'd want to know about the Hot Home feature?

options.label: How many users are clicking Hot Home listings vs non-Hot listings
options.feedback_short: Engagement first — that's mid-level thinking.
options.feedback_reveal: Click-through rate tells you the badge draws attention. It doesn't tell you whether the badge is worth acting on. A visible but unreliable badge can have great CTR right up until users stop trusting it. Engagement is a proxy for attention, not value.

options.label: Whether homes labelled Hot are actually selling faster than unlabelled ones
options.feedback_short: Prediction accuracy first — that's the senior move.
options.feedback_reveal: Before any engagement metric makes sense, you need to know if the prediction is correct. Hot Home claims a home will sell quickly. The ground truth is whether it did. If the accuracy is 40%, every engagement metric is measuring how effectively you're misleading buyers. Accuracy is the foundation — everything else layers on top.

options.label: Who decided what 'Hot' means and whether that definition should be questioned
options.feedback_short: Questioning the definition — that's staff-level thinking.
options.feedback_reveal: What exactly makes a home 'Hot'? Is it the algorithm's confidence score? Time-to-offer historically? The definition of Hot determines what the feature can ever measure. If the definition is fuzzy, the feature will never have a clean success metric. Staff PMs question the premise before measuring anything.

decision_points.prompt: You've established that accuracy matters. Whose success are you measuring — the feature serves multiple stakeholders.

options.label: Buyers primarily — they're the ones acting on the badge to schedule tours faster
options.feedback_short: Buyers only — reasonable but incomplete.
options.feedback_reveal: Buyers are the primary actors on the Hot Home signal. But sellers also have a stake in the label — a seller whose home is not marked Hot may list with a competitor. If you ignore seller perception, you'll miss a churn signal that shows up in listing supply, not buyer behaviour.

options.label: Both buyers and sellers — and their success metrics can conflict with each other
options.feedback_short: Both stakeholders, with the tension named — that's the senior answer.
options.feedback_reveal: More homes labelled Hot makes sellers happier and reduces listing churn. But it dilutes the signal for buyers and reduces prediction accuracy. A senior PM makes this tension explicit and proposes measuring both sides with the understanding that optimising one can hurt the other.

options.label: Redfin's transaction rate is the only metric that ultimately matters — the others are proxies
options.feedback_short: Transaction rate as north star — provocative and partially right.
options.feedback_reveal: Redfin makes money on closed transactions. Ultimately, if Hot Home drives more completed deals faster, it's succeeding. But this is a lagging metric — you only see it after months. A staff PM uses transaction rate as the north star while building leading indicators to detect success or failure faster.

decision_points.prompt: What's the metric most likely to tell you the feature is failing — before it shows up in transaction data?

options.label: Declining click-through rate on Hot Home badges over time
options.feedback_short: CTR decline — visible but too late.
options.feedback_reveal: Declining CTR is real but lagging. By the time aggregate CTR drops, trust has been eroding for weeks. Users don't stop clicking all at once — they stop trusting gradually and the aggregate only catches it after the damage is done.

options.label: The gap between how fast Hot Home listings sell vs non-Hot listings is narrowing
options.feedback_short: Narrowing accuracy gap — strong leading indicator.
options.feedback_reveal: If Hot Home listings are selling only marginally faster than non-Hot listings, the prediction is losing its edge. The gap in days-to-offer between Hot and non-Hot homes is a leading indicator of whether the feature still has informational value — and it shows up before CTR changes.

options.label: Repeat user behaviour — buyers who previously acted on Hot Home badges are now ignoring them
options.feedback_short: Repeat trust decay — the earliest and most accurate signal.
options.feedback_reveal: A buyer who acted on a Hot Home badge last month and got burned will not act on the next one. Cohort-level repeat action rate — users who previously engaged and whether they engage again — detects trust erosion before it appears in any aggregate metric. This is the canary in the coal mine.

calibration.strong: You're thinking at senior or staff level consistently. The next step is connecting these metrics into a decision framework — not just measuring, but defining what threshold in each metric should trigger a product change.
calibration.developing: Good instincts. The reframe that unlocks senior-level thinking: always ask what the feature claims before asking how to measure it. The claim defines the measurement.
calibration.early: You're building the right foundation. Focus on this pattern: prediction features require accuracy metrics first, engagement metrics second. Engagement without accuracy measures the wrong thing.

playbook.recap: This question is really asking whether you know the difference between measuring a feature and validating a claim — Hot Home makes a prediction, and most PMs skip straight to engagement metrics without checking if the prediction is any good.
playbook.how_to_think: When you see any prediction or recommendation feature, resist the pull toward engagement metrics. Ask first: what is the feature claiming, and is that claim correct? Accuracy is the foundation — every other metric is noise until you know the prediction works. Then ask separately for each stakeholder whether accuracy is actually creating value for them.
playbook.sticky_analogy: Measuring engagement on a prediction feature is like rating a weather app by how often people open it — not by whether the forecasts were right.
playbook.hook: Once you can measure a prediction feature well, the harder version is: what do you do when accuracy is good but engagement is still low? That's the trust problem — and it shows up in LinkedIn's connection suggestions, Spotify's Discover Weekly, and anywhere a recommendation engine has to earn belief before it can create behaviour.

episodes.title: The Three Metrics Redfin's Hot Home Actually Needs
episodes.subtitle: Accuracy, utility, and trust — why you need all three and why most measurement frameworks only get one.
episodes.youtube_description: A prediction feature has three independent success conditions: the prediction is accurate, it drives value-creating behaviour, and it builds lasting trust. Most dashboards track one. This episode shows you how to design for all three.

interactive.intro: You've established that accuracy matters. Now the question shifts to what success looks like for each stakeholder — and why their definitions can pull in opposite directions.

decision_points.prompt: You're designing the measurement framework. What's your north star metric for Hot Home?

options.label: Click-through rate on Hot Home badges — shows the feature is driving buyer action
options.feedback_short: A proxy metric — engagement without validation.
options.feedback_reveal: Click-through rate measures attention, not value. A buyer who clicks a Hot Home badge out of FOMO and then loses the house to another buyer hasn't been served well. This metric optimises for the feature looking busy, not for it being useful.

options.label: Days-to-offer gap between Hot Home and non-Hot Home listings
options.feedback_short: Strong north star — this measures the prediction's actual value.
options.feedback_reveal: If Hot Home listings close meaningfully faster than comparable non-Hot listings, the feature is doing its job: helping buyers act on real urgency signals. Building on the accuracy-first framing from the Product Thinking episode, this metric directly operationalises prediction validity — it's accuracy measured through outcomes, not through a separate test.

options.label: Completed transactions attributable to Hot Home-influenced behaviour
options.feedback_short: The right north star, but too lagging for fast iteration.
options.feedback_reveal: Transaction rate is ultimately what Redfin cares about. But it lags by months — you can't course-correct a feature in weekly sprints if your success metric takes a quarter to move. Staff PMs use this as the ultimate arbiter while building faster-moving leading indicators like the days-to-offer gap to make the feedback loop usable.

decision_points.prompt: Your north star drops by 15% week over week. What's your first diagnostic move?

options.label: Check if total Hot Home badge volume changed — maybe fewer listings are being labelled
options.feedback_short: Volume first is a reasonable instinct, but it skips the most important question.
options.feedback_reveal: Volume changes explain the how, not the why. If fewer listings are labelled Hot, you still need to know whether that's a labelling threshold change, a market slowdown, or an algorithm shift. Building on the north star thinking from the last question — a drop in the days-to-offer gap needs to be traced to whether the prediction model drifted, not just whether fewer badges were shown.

options.label: Separate the drop by buyer cohort — new users vs returning users behaving differently
options.feedback_short: Cohort separation is the senior diagnostic move.
options.feedback_reveal: New users don't have a trust baseline for Hot Home — their behaviour is driven by the badge's face value. Returning users who've acted on Hot Home before have a learned response based on whether it worked for them. If returning users are driving the drop, that's a trust erosion signal. If new users are driving it, that's a discoverability or awareness problem. Same aggregate number, completely different root causes.

options.label: Check if the underlying prediction model accuracy has changed in the same period
options.feedback_short: Root cause first — this is the staff-level diagnostic instinct.
options.feedback_reveal: If prediction accuracy dropped before the north star dropped, you have a model drift problem and everything downstream is a symptom. Checking accuracy first means you're diagnosing the mechanism, not the symptom. This is the same discipline from the Product Thinking episode applied to diagnosis: the feature makes a claim, and the claim's validity is always the first thing to verify.

calibration.strong: You're thinking at senior or staff level — you're measuring outcomes, not activity, and diagnosing at the mechanism level. In the Execution episode, we'll turn this into a prioritisation framework for what to actually build first.
calibration.developing: Good instincts on the measurement side. The shift that unlocks the next level: always trace metrics back to the claim the feature is making. Does your metric actually test whether the claim is correct?
calibration.early: You're building the foundation. The pattern to internalise: engagement metrics measure activity, not value. For prediction features, value means the prediction is accurate and users act on it correctly.

playbook.recap: This question is really asking whether you can design a measurement system that tests the feature's core claim — not just tracks how much it's used.
playbook.how_to_think: Build your metric stack in layers: accuracy at the base (is the prediction correct?), utility in the middle (does accuracy create value for each stakeholder?), and trust at the top (does repeated accuracy build lasting behaviour change?). Diagnostic questions flow down the stack — when a top-level metric drops, start by checking the layer below it.
playbook.sticky_analogy: A good metrics framework for a prediction feature is like a diagnostic tree for a car engine warning light — you don't fix the light, you trace the signal to its source.
playbook.hook: Once you can measure a prediction feature well, the harder version is designing the feedback loop so the model improves over time — how does user behaviour become training signal without creating a filter bubble?

episodes.title: What to Build First When the Hot Home Badge Is Failing
episodes.subtitle: The explicit call most candidates avoid — what to instrument first and what to cut.
episodes.youtube_description: You've diagnosed the problem. Now what do you actually build? This episode walks through the prioritisation decision for a prediction feature — what to instrument first, what to cut, and the framework that works across any recommendation or prediction product.

interactive.intro: You've defined success and built the measurement framework. Now the hardest question: given real constraints, what do you actually build first — and what do you explicitly cut?

decision_points.prompt: You have 2 engineers and one quarter. The prediction accuracy is reasonable but trust metrics show buyers are losing confidence over time. What do you build first?

options.label: Improve the Hot Home algorithm to increase prediction accuracy
options.feedback_short: Improving accuracy is valuable — but it may not solve the trust problem.
options.feedback_reveal: Better accuracy helps if inaccuracy is the root cause of eroding trust. But if buyers have lost confidence because they can't tell HOW confident the model is — not just whether it's right — then a more accurate opaque model won't rebuild trust. You're solving the right problem only if you've correctly diagnosed why trust is dropping.

options.label: Add a confidence signal to the badge — show buyers how strong the prediction is
options.feedback_short: This addresses trust directly, not just accuracy.
options.feedback_reveal: Building on the trust layer from the metrics work — buyers don't just need accurate predictions, they need enough information to calibrate their own confidence. A strong/moderate/weak signal (or a predicted days-to-offer range) turns a binary badge into a navigational tool. This is a product change, not a model change, and it can be shipped with 2 engineers in a quarter. It also creates better training signal because buyers who see confidence levels self-select, improving the feedback loop.

options.label: Build seller-facing transparency — show sellers why their home was or wasn't labelled Hot
options.feedback_short: Addresses the supply-side trust problem most candidates miss.
options.feedback_reveal: The metrics framework surfaced a seller tension: homes not labelled Hot may churn to competitors. Seller transparency — 'your home wasn't labelled Hot because days-on-market in your zip code averages 45 days, and Hot requires under 14' — reduces the perception that the label is arbitrary or biased. This is lower priority than the buyer confidence signal unless seller churn is measurably accelerating, but a staff PM would name the cut explicitly: 'I'm deferring seller transparency until we confirm buyer trust is the dominant problem.'

decision_points.prompt: What's the one thing you would explicitly NOT build this quarter, and why?

options.label: A 'Watch List' feature letting buyers save Hot Home alerts for later review
options.feedback_short: Reasonable cut — but the reasoning matters more than the answer.
options.feedback_reveal: Cutting a Watch List is defensible if the current problem is trust erosion, not feature gap. But if you're cutting it because 'it's not the priority,' you haven't really defended the cut — you've just deferred the decision. The senior version of this answer names the specific opportunity cost: 'A Watch List optimises for engagement, and we've established that engagement without accuracy is measuring the wrong thing.'

options.label: A/B testing framework for comparing badge variants
options.feedback_short: Correct cut, and the reasoning is structurally sound.
options.feedback_reveal: A/B testing infrastructure is generically valuable but it's a platform investment, not a trust fix. The constraint here is two engineers and one quarter — that's not enough to build reliable experimentation infrastructure AND ship the confidence signal. Name the cut clearly: 'I'm deferring A/B infrastructure because we need a trust signal faster than experimentation overhead will allow. We'll validate the confidence signal with a holdout and commit to building test infrastructure next quarter.'

options.label: Expanding Hot Home to more listing categories (rentals, new construction)
options.feedback_short: Strong cut — you're protecting the core before expanding the surface area.
options.feedback_reveal: Expanding scope when the existing feature has a trust problem is how products accumulate surface area without depth. A staff PM makes this cut and defends it with a principle: 'We shouldn't expand the blast radius of a feature whose prediction validity we can't yet fully explain to users. Fix the trust loop on the existing surface first, then expand.' This is the staff-level pattern — knowing when NOT to grow.

calibration.strong: You're making explicit cuts with principled reasoning — not just prioritising, but defending what gets deferred and why. That's the senior-to-staff transition in execution thinking.
calibration.developing: Your instincts on what to build are sound. The upgrade: make the cut explicit every time. Senior PMs don't just say what they're building — they name what they're NOT building and defend the decision.
calibration.early: Good foundation. The pattern to build: every prioritisation decision has two parts — what you're doing, and what you're deliberately not doing. The second part is where judgment lives.

playbook.recap: This question is really asking whether you can make a defensible prioritisation call under real constraints — and whether you have the clarity to name what you're cutting and why.
playbook.how_to_think: When you face an execution question, start from the diagnosis you've already done. What does the measurement framework tell you the root cause is? Build toward that root cause, not toward the most visible symptom. Then name one explicit cut — not because it's unimportant, but because the constraint forces a choice and pretending otherwise isn't a strategy.
playbook.sticky_analogy: Prioritisation without explicit cuts is like packing for a trip by listing everything you want to bring — the discipline is in what you leave behind, not what you include.
playbook.hook: Once you can make defensible cuts in a single-feature context, the harder version is cross-team prioritisation — when the cut you're making affects another team's roadmap. How do you make that call without authority?
